# Chapter 09

[l0perei1] The Shelby sat inside the shade bay like an animal that knew it had been stolen. Dustless. Chrome clean enough to show Flynn’s face as a warped coin. The desert light stopped at the threshold and the air inside tasted of old coolant and oil that had never really been burned. Cage walked around it slow, hands behind his back, smiling like a priest.

[g5skqeyh] They’d dragged it home under tarps and silence, the rat-rods and the solar trailers tucked around it like embarrassed cousins. For two days nobody touched it. Even Birdie kept his hands off, pacing, eyes too wide. Wrench sat on a tire and smoked and watched the sky, as if the sky was going to call in the theft.

[hbwo4s1q] Diesel broke the spell by rolling her cart up and opening the hood with the gentleness of a surgeon. The latch clicked like a small gun. She leaned in. She did not say beautiful. She never said that word. She traced the lines with a thumb and started making a list in her head, eyes moving like they were reading.

[t2cefs6e] Flynn learned that a car that’s been preserved is still a car that’s been abandoned. Rubber hardens. Seals dry. Gas turns to varnish. The collectors keep the temperature and the lights and the scent, but they don’t keep the motion. Diesel pulled hoses and they cracked in her hands. She tossed them into a bin with no ceremony.

[nhiiubz6] Cage wanted it running right now. That afternoon. He stood close behind Diesel, breathing on her neck, and talked about Mexico like it was already happening. He said the name of the race softly, like prayer. La Carrera del Chile Rojo. Flynn watched Diesel’s shoulders tighten and watched Cage not notice.

[rwy7txt5] Diesel told him no. Not loud. Not defiant. Just no, like gravity. She set the pace and the pace was measured in gaskets, in torque specs, in waiting for a part to cool before you touched it. Cage laughed and flicked ash and went to the far end of the bay to sharpen a knife on a stone like the sound could hurry time.

[h4vnqfpn] The compound changed with the Shelby there. It felt less like a camp and more like a staging ground. Fuel drums multiplied. The solar array got cleaned twice in a week. Birdie marked maps on disposable screens and then tore them up and marked them again. Flynn found himself listening for drones even when the sky was empty.

[z0g3agw3] At night the car’s silhouette lay under its cover like a body. Flynn would wake and see it in the dark and think of the guard’s face from Phoenix, the blood on his teeth, the way Cage’s hands had kept moving after the man stopped. The cover didn’t hide that. Nothing hid that. It just made the shape quieter.

[s8hh91yu] Diesel worked on it like it was penance. She stripped the intake and the old smell of gasoline came up sweet and rotten. She cleaned jets with wire. She set the timing by feel and by ear, listening to the imagined engine in her head. Flynn held the light and tried not to ask questions that sounded like worship.

[5a3u3ngb] She showed him the heart of it. Big block. Old-school violence cast in iron. She tapped the valve cover and said this is history, kid. Not in museums. In here. Then she shut the hood and told him history will kill you if you treat it like a story. Her hands were steady. Her eyes were tired.

[48q41kcj] Birdie’s job was fuel. He said it like it was a game, like he was going to steal candy. He had a contact in a border town who ran a generator warehouse, old diesel units and forbidden pump rigs. Birdie left before dawn on an e-bike with a false plate and came back smelling like cigarette smoke and cheap cologne.

[gsusu7bo] Flynn watched him unload jerry cans like they were treasure. Real gasoline. Not the home-brew oil they cooked in drums. It had weight to it, a seriousness. Birdie’s face glowed with pride and fear, like he’d walked into a room full of men and come back out with his skin still on.

[6b7yg5h4] Cage poured a little into a glass jar and held it up to the sun. It was pale and clear. He shook it and listened. Like you listen to a snake. He said, This is the only thing they couldn’t ban. Fire. He handed the jar to Flynn and for a second Flynn felt the warm slickness of it through the glass and felt ashamed.

[3vr7ggh7] They tested the fuel in a lawn mower engine first. Diesel insisted. Cage rolled his eyes. The engine coughed and then caught, spitting blue smoke and noise into the morning air. Birdie whooped. The sound drew a couple of coyotes up on the ridge. They stood and watched, ears forward, like the noise was a challenge.

[h3o67w1c] Wrench didn’t cheer. He stood apart with his arms crossed, face set, looking at the horizon. Flynn went to him with a wrench in his hand just to have an excuse. Wrench said, You know what makes a man quiet? When he’s counting ahead. Flynn asked what he was counting. Wrench didn’t answer.

[h0bi7qx2] The Shelby’s engine came out on day four. Diesel and Flynn and Birdie on the hoist chain, Cage pretending to help, hands clean. The block hung there like a monument, dripping old oil in slow drops. Diesel wiped the pan and pointed to metal glitter. She said, See that. That’s time.

[onxln1je] Flynn expected Cage to get angry. Cage just stared at the glitter and nodded, as if it confirmed something he already knew. He said they’d rebuild it stronger. He said Don Castellanos would expect them to show up with blood on the bumper and lightning under the hood. He looked at Flynn when he said blood, like he was sharing a joke.

[48c8p47v] Diesel ordered parts through a dead man’s account. Flynn watched the transaction flicker on the disposable screen. The system asked for a voiceprint and she played a recording. It accepted. Somewhere a warehouse robot began moving boxes for a ghost. Flynn felt the world’s convenience turn sick inside him.

[805gma8t] While they waited, Diesel cleaned. Not just the engine bay. Everything. She sanded corrosion off connectors, chased threads, replaced brittle wire. Flynn learned the difference between salvage and restoration. Salvage was hunger. Restoration was obsession. The Shelby demanded obsession and Cage fed on it.

[n89b80wo] Cage spent those days in motion. He did pushups in the dust until his scars turned raw. He paced the perimeter with binoculars. He talked to nobody, then suddenly he’d talk for ten minutes straight about the race route in Mexico, about the turns that would separate boys from drivers. He said, There’s a place where the road drops and you can feel God let go.

[rblaq36w] Flynn tried to picture it and couldn’t. He pictured the guard instead. He pictured the guard being revived under fluorescent lights with ads playing in his skull, being told he was lucky. Cage had said no harm done. Flynn had believed him because it was easier than admitting he’d watched a man get dismantled.

[670lzpjg] One evening Diesel had Flynn stay late with her, the bay door half shut against the heat. Birdie was out riding the perimeter and Cage was nowhere. Diesel loosened a bolt and then sat back on her heels and looked at Flynn like she was deciding whether to tell him something true.

[gbhoa5pi] She said, When I met him he wasn’t a leader. He was just a kid with a wrecked Nova and too much anger. He’d grin and say sorry after he broke something. He’d mean it, I think. Now he grins and he doesn’t say sorry. She wiped grease across her forearm like she could erase the years.

[1bav7un3] Flynn said Cage saved him. He said the compound saved him. He heard himself talk like Birdie. Diesel didn’t argue. She just nodded like you nod at a drunk telling you he’s fine. She said, I’m not telling you to leave. I’m telling you to keep your eyes open. You can love a machine and still know it will throw a rod.

[yp1fw34p] A wind came up and pushed hot air through the cracks. The cover over the Shelby snapped softly. Diesel listened to it like it was language. She said, Watch Birdie too. He thinks this is a movie. He thinks revival means reset. Nobody tells him what it feels like when the bones heal wrong and the nightmares don’t heal at all.

[45etq9x5] Flynn asked why she stayed. The question came out before he could stop it. Diesel stared at her hands. She said, Because I’m good at this. Because it’s the only honest work I ever did. Because I like the sound an engine makes when it’s right. She paused. Then she said, And because if I go, there’s nothing here between him and the kid.

[tjjbup5d] The parts arrived in plain brown crates on a drone drop. Flynn carried them inside and felt ridiculous gratitude toward the same system that would arrest them if it knew. Diesel unpacked bearings like they were jewelry. She laid them out on clean rags. Cage finally came in, eyes bright, and said, Tonight.

[wvql3yvo] Diesel didn’t look up. She said, Not tonight. Cage’s smile held. He leaned on the fender and watched her work, watching the way her wrists turned, the way she knew the torque without looking at the wrench. Flynn saw hunger in Cage that had nothing to do with cars. Like he wanted to consume her skill.

[d8pe0jus] Cage said, You worry too much. Diesel said, You don’t worry enough. Birdie wandered in mid-argument and froze, sensing weather. Cage softened instantly. He ruffled Birdie’s hair like a father. He said, We’re building a legend. Birdie nodded like he’d been chosen for something sacred.

[enx3y846] They bolted the engine back together over two days. Diesel moved slow and exact. Flynn learned to read her silence. When she was quiet, it was focus. When she was too quiet, it was anger. Cage kept trying to speed it up, suggesting shortcuts. Diesel ignored him like he was a radio ad you couldn’t fully turn off.

[pxd253mg] The first start happened at dawn. Birdie and Flynn on either side, Diesel in front with a fire extinguisher, Cage in the driver’s seat because of course he was. He turned the key and the starter whined. It didn’t catch. Diesel reached in and adjusted the choke. Cage turned again.

[c7f9gpyh] The engine coughed once, then lit, and the bay filled with sound so thick you could taste it. The idle was rough at first and then it smoothed, deep and steady, the kind of sound that made your chest hurt with recognition. Birdie laughed out loud. Flynn felt tears start and hated himself for it.

[e4hk7c9b] Cage revved it hard, not caring about break-in, not caring about anything but noise. Diesel slapped the hood and yelled at him to ease it. Cage ignored her, eyes half closed, listening. Flynn saw the same look that had been on Cage’s face when he hit the guard. Pleasure. Pure.

[77xinmo0] Diesel cut the ignition herself by reaching in and yanking the coil wire. The engine died with a violent shudder. Cage snapped his head around, furious for a second, then he smiled like he respected her for it. He said, You’re scared. Diesel said, I’m smart. Big difference.

[nwcv21tv] The test drive had to wait until night. Too much surveillance in daylight. They rolled the Shelby out under a sky full of stars and heat lightning far away. Flynn slid into the passenger seat and the interior smelled like leather and dust and a rich man’s cologne that wouldn’t leave.

[kuq6hvy2] Cage drove like the car was a weapon he’d just found. Smooth at first, then harder. The speed came up fast, the old needle climbing like it was trying to run away. Flynn watched Cage’s hands on the wheel. Calm. Loose. Like he was holding someone’s throat with two fingers.

[em6gq30k] They took the frontage road past the solar farm and out onto the broken highway where the cameras had died years ago. Cage opened it up. The Shelby surged and the whole world narrowed to headlight beam and asphalt seam. Flynn felt his implant buzz at the edge of his skull, warning him about tach and heart rate like a nanny.

[1h1l2y33] Cage laughed and turned the warning off. He said, This is what the pods took from you. This sound. This risk. Flynn wanted to agree. He wanted to be worthy of the seat. But he kept seeing Wrench’s face in the firelight, counting ahead, and Diesel’s eyes, heavy with something like grief.

[tdbp9fw2] When they returned, Diesel was waiting with her arms folded. She listened to the engine tick as it cooled and she watched the fresh rubber on the rear tires. She said, You cooked it. Cage said, It’s fine. Diesel looked at Flynn and said, Tell me what you heard. Flynn hesitated, then told her about a faint knock.

[6vn4rf35] Diesel nodded once, sharp. She said, We fix it tomorrow. Cage started to argue and Diesel cut him off. She said, If you want to die in Mexico, do it in the race, not in my shop. Cage stared at her for a long time. Then he stepped close enough that Flynn thought he might hit her. He didn’t. He whispered something Flynn couldn’t hear.

[2hxz8f7h] After Cage walked away, Diesel leaned against the fender and closed her eyes. Flynn asked what Cage said. Diesel opened her eyes and they were flat. She said, He said he’s tired of people acting like his life belongs to them. Like they have a claim on him. She wiped her hands and said, That’s the closest he gets to a warning.

[yoa92ojh] The next day a message arrived through the underground channels, a video that played on Birdie’s screen and then self-erased. Don Castellanos in a white linen shirt, behind him endless green rows of chilli plants under glass. He smiled like a man who owned weather. He spoke about freedom and danger and art.

[d4xes2jd] Birdie watched it three times. Cage watched it once and then went still, as if listening to a distant engine. Flynn watched Diesel while the speech played. Diesel’s mouth tightened on certain words. Death. Authenticity. Remembered. She’d heard the pitch before, in Cage’s campfire sermons, and she knew what it hid.

[m63hyjfp] When the video ended, Birdie asked when they’d leave. Cage said soon. Diesel said after the car is right. Cage didn’t look at her. He looked at Flynn. He said, You’re going to drive it. Flynn felt his stomach drop. The offer was a hook. He could feel it in his bones.

[st3zaohd] Flynn nodded anyway. He told himself he could handle it. He told himself he’d be careful. He told himself he’d keep Birdie safe. The lies stacked neatly like parts on a bench. Cage clapped him on the shoulder, a touch that was almost affectionate, and said, Good. We’re not going to Mexico to participate.

[7zo2j6mo] That night Flynn lay on his cot and listened to the desert settle. Somewhere out in the dark a generator coughed. The Shelby’s metal pinged as it cooled. He thought about the guard waking up with a new scar and an ad in his ear telling him to buy better insurance. He thought about how easy it was to call that mercy.

[ctqkwt3n] Near midnight Diesel came out of the bay and sat on the step beside him without asking. She didn’t smoke. She just sat. After a while she said, Car’s almost ready. That means he’s almost ready. Flynn didn’t answer. Diesel said, You don’t have to be like him to survive him. But you do have to see him.

[z7ahyvq9] Flynn stared up at the stars and tried to see the outline of the future the way Cage saw it, bright and simple and violent. All he could see was heat and distance and the thin line between going fast and coming apart. In the bay the Shelby waited, quiet, patient, ready to turn gasoline into noise and consequence.
